question: Strategy execution fails most commonly for which reason?
options: Strategies are always poorly designed and need better analysis

Implementation failures: poor strategy execution is more common than poor strategy design; McKinsey research: 70% of transformations fail, primarily due to implementation rather than strategy design problems; the most common execution failures: (1) No clear link between strategy and daily decisions — frontline employees don't know what the strategy means for their work; (2) Resource allocation doesn't match stated priorities — companies say innovation is the priority but allocate 95% of budget to the core business; (3) No clear ownership for strategic initiatives — multiple people share accountability for strategic projects, meaning no one is personally accountable; (4) Strategy never operationalized into measurable milestones — strategy as aspiration without operational translation
Implementation always succeeds when the strategy is well-designed
Only large companies have strategy implementation challenges
explanation: The strategy-execution gap: Larry Bossidy and Ram Charan ('Execution: The Discipline of Getting Things Done') identified execution as the primary differentiator between successful and unsuccessful strategies. Their research: 'Most strategies fail not because the strategy itself was flawed, but because the organization couldn't execute it.' The execution fundamentals: (1) Know your people (right people in right roles for the strategy); (2) Know your business (understand operational reality, not just financial projections); (3) Set priorities (ruthlessly few, not a 20-item strategic agenda); (4) Link people, strategy, and operations (compensation, resource allocation, and performance metrics must all reinforce the strategic priorities). The most common evidence of execution failure: a company's budgets and people assignments look nothing like the stated strategic priorities — the organizational reality proves what the organization actually cares about, regardless of what the strategy documents say.

question: Mckinsey's 7-S Framework identifies which seven organizational elements that must align for strategy implementation to succeed?
options: Sales, Service, Supply, Systems, Structure, Staff, Sustainability

Strategy, Structure, Systems, Staff, Skills, Style, and Shared Values — all starting with 'S'; the framework argues: changing strategy without aligning the other six elements fails; example: a company adopts a customer-centricity strategy but maintains functional organizational structures (misaligned structure), silo-reinforcing compensation systems (misaligned systems), and managers who believe the product is more important than the customer (misaligned style and shared values) → strategy doesn't change how the organization operates because the other elements don't change
Strengths, Strategies, Stakeholders, Suppliers, Systems, Style, Staff
Strategy, Structure, Scope, Scale, Systems, Speed, Synergy
explanation: 7-S Framework (McKinsey, 1980) application: IBM's transformation from hardware company to services company (2000s): Strategy changed (from hardware to services). Structure had to change (from product divisions to industry/services teams). Systems had to change (account management systems, project management vs. product inventory systems). Staff had to change (needed consultants and solution architects, not hardware engineers). Skills had to change (develop industry expertise and integration skills, not manufacturing skills). Style had to change (from transactional sales to consultative partnerships). Shared Values had to change (from 'we build the best hardware' to 'we solve business problems'). IBM's transformation took 15+ years and several rounds of restructuring — illustrating that aligned organizational change is far harder than announcing a new strategy. Changing one S without the others is why most transformations fail.

question: John Kotter's 8-Step Change Model identifies establishing urgency as the first step in change management. Why is urgency the prerequisite for all subsequent change steps?
options: Urgency is the last step in change management, not the first

Without urgency, people don't mobilize to change: organizations have enormous inertia — existing processes, relationships, power structures, and habits are stable and self-reinforcing; change requires disruption of these equilibria; people change when the pain of staying the same exceeds the discomfort of changing; 'establishing urgency' means: helping organizational members understand why the status quo is not an option (competitive threat, financial pressure, market change, missed opportunity); without genuine urgency, change initiatives compete with business-as-usual and lose — business-as-usual has the organizational inertia advantage; urgency creates the 'burning platform' (change or fall off the platform) that motivates the organizational energy needed for change
Urgency is counterproductive and creates resistance to change
People are always ready to change and urgency is unnecessary
explanation: Kotter's 8 steps: (1) Establish urgency; (2) Create a guiding coalition; (3) Develop vision and strategy; (4) Communicate the change vision; (5) Empower broad action; (6) Generate short-term wins; (7) Consolidate gains; (8) Anchor changes in culture. The urgency paradox: companies most in need of change (those facing serious competitive threats) often have leaders most resistant to admitting the urgency — acknowledging that the current strategy is failing implies that past leadership decisions were wrong. Leaders who genuinely create urgency (Lou Gerstner's first words as IBM CEO in 1993: 'IBM is in crisis') face personal credibility risk. The alternative (gentle messages about 'continuous improvement') doesn't create the energy needed for genuine transformation. IBM's near-death experience in 1993 ($8B loss, potential breakup) was the urgency that enabled Gerstner's transformation.

question: A CEO announces a strategic transformation: 'We will become a customer-centric organization. Customer satisfaction is our top priority.' After six months: the company's bonus system is unchanged (still rewards revenue, not satisfaction), the organizational structure is unchanged (product-centric divisions), and frontline employees still resolve customer complaints by following the existing script. What has been omitted from the strategy implementation?
options: The CEO's announcement was too brief and required a longer speech

Structural and incentive alignment — strategy without implementation mechanisms is aspiration: announcing strategic intent without changing the reinforcement mechanisms is a common and predictable failure; the 'customer-centric' transformation requires: (1) Metric change: add customer NPS and satisfaction to the balanced scorecard; (2) Compensation change: tie management bonus to customer metrics; (3) Structural change: possibly create a Chief Customer Officer role or rebalance product vs. customer teams; (4) Process change: empower frontline employees to resolve customer issues beyond the script (what is the actual authority frontline employees have?); (5) Decision rights change: when customer satisfaction and product revenue conflict, which wins?; the organizational reality test: 'do the systems and structure reinforce the stated strategy?' — if they don't, the strategy isn't real yet
Communicating strategy repeatedly is the only necessary implementation action
Strategy announcements automatically change employee behavior without additional changes
explanation: The McKinsey organizational health framework: organizations that achieve strategic transformation change: structure + systems + people + culture together — not just the stated strategy. The 'strategy announcement only' failure: the most common form of pseudo-implementation. An employee trying to be customer-centric in a system that measures and rewards revenue: faces cognitive dissonance (told to prioritize customers; rewarded for sales), resource conflict (customer service time reduces sales time), and social pressure from colleagues (who are rewarded for revenue and may see customer focus as misaligned priorities). The implementation equation: stated strategy + aligned incentives + aligned structure + aligned skills = actual strategy. Any element missing produces the behavior the missing element incentivizes, not the behavior the strategy states. 'Culture eats strategy for breakfast' often means 'the old incentive structure eats the new strategy announcement for breakfast.'

question: Walmart's early digital commerce strategy (2015–2019 investment period) involved acquiring Jet.com for $3.3B (2016), acquiring Shoebuy, Bonobos, Moosejaw, and Modcloth, and building Walmart.com capabilities. The results were mixed. What does Walmart's digital execution challenge reveal about strategy implementation in legacy organizations?
options: Legacy organizations are always incapable of executing digital strategies

The innovator's dilemma operationalized as implementation challenge: Walmart's structural challenge: (1) Culture conflict — 'EDLP' (Every Day Low Price) culture vs. Jet.com's premium brand acquisitions (Bonobos, Modcloth served different customers than Walmart's price-sensitive base); (2) Technology architecture debt — decades of legacy retail systems (point-of-sale, inventory, supply chain) were not designed for e-commerce integration; retrofitting them is harder than building new; (3) Human capital mismatch — Walmart's talent base (retail operations, logistics) is different from e-commerce talent (user experience, data science, digital marketing); (4) Execution scale challenge: Amazon's e-commerce was built from scratch; Walmart is trying to transform a 10,000-store retail operation while simultaneously building e-commerce; (5) Channel conflict — Walmart's store operators resist online cannibalization; the solution: Walmart eventually invested $15B+ in e-commerce, hired Marc Lore (Jet.com founder) to lead digital, and reorganized to integrate e-commerce with store operations — partial success but still trailing Amazon
Acquisitions are always the correct strategy for digital transformation
Digital transformation in retail is impossible for traditional companies
explanation: Walmart's digital transformation execution: by 2024, Walmart's e-commerce had grown to $70B+ in annual US sales — significant scale but still well behind Amazon (~$370B). The implementation insight: Walmart's most successful e-commerce strategies leveraged its physical store network (omnichannel advantages Amazon can't replicate): same-day grocery delivery from stores, buy-online-pickup-in-store (BOPIS), returns at any store. This 'stores as fulfillment centers' strategy is a unique competitive position for Walmart that a pure-play e-commerce company like Amazon can't easily match. The implementation lesson: the best strategy for an incumbent isn't to replicate the disruptor's approach — it's to leverage the incumbent's unique assets (10,000 stores = last-mile delivery infrastructure) in a way the disruptor can't. The strategy implementation challenge: realizing this strategic insight required changing store operators' incentives (rewarding store managers for e-commerce pickup volume, not fighting it).

question: Johnson & Johnson's decentralized management model has 250+ operating companies, each with significant autonomy. When J&J decided to develop COVID-19 vaccines (Janssen, 2020), they had to coordinate across R&D, manufacturing, regulatory affairs, and commercial units globally at unprecedented speed. What strategy implementation challenges does this structure create?
options: J&J's decentralized model is ideal for all strategic initiatives regardless of coordination requirements

Coordination-autonomy tension in decentralized organizations: J&J's decentralized model creates: (1) Accountability clarity (each OpCo owns its results — no diffuse responsibility); (2) Entrepreneurial culture (OpCo leaders behave like business owners); (3) Speed in normal operations (don't need corporate approval for most decisions). COVID-19 vaccine challenge required: (1) Global coordination across OpCos that normally operate independently; (2) Resource allocation across geographic units (manufacturing capacity in the Netherlands, Belgium, US, South Africa) that normally compete for capital; (3) Regulatory strategy coordination across 150+ country submissions simultaneously; (4) Supply chain integration beyond any single OpCo's scope. J&J's response: created a dedicated cross-company coordinating structure (essentially a temporary centralized overlay on the decentralized base structure) to manage the vaccine program — preserving the decentralized model for ongoing business while centralizing the extraordinary coordination requirement; the strategic implementation lesson: organizational structure determines normal-state coordination capacity; extraordinary initiatives may require structural overlays
J&J should have permanently centralized all operations to handle COVID response
Decentralized organizations always outperform centralized ones in crisis situations
explanation: Ambidextrous organizations (O'Reilly and Tushman): organizations need to balance exploitation (optimizing current business) with exploration (developing new capabilities and markets). J&J's decentralized model optimizes for exploitation (each OpCo maximizes its market opportunity) but creates exploration coordination challenges (cross-OpCo initiatives require deliberate mechanisms). The temporary centralization approach: many large organizations create 'task forces,' 'ventures,' or 'special programs' as structural overlays for high-priority initiatives that require cross-unit coordination beyond normal operations. NASA's Apollo program: NASA's normal structure was research-focused; the Apollo program required a temporary operational overlay that centralized manufacturing, testing, and launch coordination. The implementation principle: the normal organizational structure should optimize for the dominant business; extraordinary strategic initiatives require dedicated structural mechanisms that aren't permanently embedded in the normal structure.

question: Intel's failed attempt to enter the mobile chip market (2007–2015) illustrates that having a technology solution is not sufficient for strategy implementation. Intel's mobile chips (Atom) had competitive specifications but lost to Qualcomm. What implementation factors explain Intel's mobile failure?
options: Intel's mobile chips were technically inferior to all competitors' products

Ecosystem, business model, and organizational commitment failures: Intel's challenges: (1) Ecosystem gap — the mobile ecosystem (Android app developers, OEM relationships, carrier relationships) was built around ARM architecture; switching to Intel chips required app re-compilation and ecosystem partner realignment that few OEMs were willing to undertake without a compelling advantage; (2) Business model mismatch — Intel's traditional business model: sell chips to PC OEMs at high margins; mobile business model: sell chips to smartphone OEMs at much lower margins, potentially subsidizing chip prices to gain share; Intel's financial model didn't support the mobile margin structure; (3) Manufacturing cost structure — Intel's fabs (built for power-hungry PC chips) weren't optimized for mobile's power-efficiency requirements initially; (4) Organizational commitment — Intel's profit center (PC chips) had structural incentives to resist resources going to mobile (cannibalization of margins); (5) Speed-to-customer — Qualcomm's integrated modem+application processor (baseband + AP) provided OEMs one supplier instead of two; Intel's solution required separate modem sourcing
Intel's mobile failure was caused by patent restrictions on mobile technology
Intel lacked the financial resources to invest in mobile chip development
explanation: Intel's mobile failure case study: Intel's CEO Paul Otellini acknowledged (in a 2013 Atlantic interview) that Intel turned down the original iPhone chip contract because Apple's price point couldn't generate the margins Intel required. Qualcomm had built the entire mobile ecosystem (LTE modem + application processor in one chip, carrier relationships, Android OEM relationships, CDMA/WCDMA patents) that Intel would need years to replicate. The organizational structure failure: Intel's organizational incentives were aligned around PC chip margins (60%+); mobile chip business required accepting 10–20% margins to win design wins. The PC processor group leadership had no incentive to support margin-destroying mobile investment. By the time Intel committed (2012), the mobile market was essentially set: all major Android OEMs had 3-year Qualcomm design cycles. Intel eventually exited mobile chips entirely (2016). The implementation lesson: even technically capable companies fail when business model requirements are incompatible with the core business's incentive structure.
